text.title: mq-patterns Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for mq-patterns. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Before writing any queue code, state the topology explicitly in a contract document: queue name, schema, partition key, ordering requirement, retry policy, DLQ target, and idempotency strategy. The contract is the primary deliverable; code is secondary.
rule.rationale: Topology decisions have long tails. A schema change or retry-policy change in production requires explicit reviewer approval. Writing the contract first surfaces these decisions while they are cheap to change.

rule.statement: Select the topology based on the consumer relationship: one message, one consumer (work queue); one message, many independent consumers (fan-out); async call expecting a reply (request/reply); all others routed after max failures (DLQ as complement to any of the above).
rule.rationale: Mixing topologies without explicit intent leads to messages processed by the wrong consumer set or correlation IDs lost between services.

rule.statement: Design every consumer for idempotency. Messages may be delivered more than once under at-least-once delivery semantics; the consumer must produce the same side-effect on the first and every subsequent delivery of the same message.
rule.rationale: No major broker guarantees exactly-once delivery without application-level cooperation. Building idempotency into the consumer is cheaper and more reliable than trusting broker-level deduplication.

rule.statement: Every queue that can receive a failure must have a DLQ configured before the queue goes live. DLQ depth must be wired to an alert.
rule.rationale: Without a DLQ, failed messages are either lost or retry forever. A DLQ without an alert is equivalent to no DLQ — messages accumulate silently and business logic stops working.

rule.statement: Monitor queue depth, consumer lag (Kafka), DLQ size, oldest-unacked-age, and processing error rate. These metrics are the earliest incident signals for distributed systems.
rule.rationale: A growing queue depth indicates the consumer cannot keep up. DLQ growth indicates systematic failures. Without these metrics, incidents surface only after user-visible impact.
